entries.title: St. Justin Martyr
entries.subtitle: c. 100 - c. 165 · Greek
entries.body: Eve, a virgin and undefiled, having conceived the word of the serpent, brought forth disobedience and death. But the Virgin Mary, receiving faith and joy when the angel Gabriel announced to her the good tidings, answered: Be it done unto me according to thy word. Εὕα γὰρ παρθένος καὶ ἄφθορος... ἀκούσασα δὲ τὸν λόγον τὸν ἀπὸ τοῦ ὄφεως ἔτεκε παρακοὴν καὶ θάνατον· πίστιν δὲ καὶ χαρὰν λαβοῦσα Μαρία ἡ παρθένος... ἀπεκρίνατο· γένοιτό μοι κατὰ τὸ ῥῆμά σου. Dialogue with Trypho, ch. 100.

entries.title: St. Irenaeus of Lyons
entries.subtitle: c. 130 - c. 202 · Greek, surviving in Latin
entries.body: Just as that one [Eve] was seduced by the word of an angel to flee from God, transgressing His word, so this one [Mary] was evangelized by the word of an angel that she might bear God, obeying His word. And while the former had disobeyed God, the latter was persuaded to obey God, that the Virgin Mary might become the advocate [advocata] of the virgin Eve. Sicut illa per angeli sermonem seducta est ut effugeret Deum praevaricata verbum eius, ita et haec per angelicum sermonem evangelizata est ut portaret Deum obediens eius verbo. Et si ea inobedierat Deo, sed haec suasa est obedire Deo, uti virginis Evae virgo Maria fieret advocata. Adversus Haereses V.19.1.

entries.title: Tertullian of Carthage
entries.subtitle: c. 155 - c. 220 · Latin
entries.body: Eve believed the serpent; Mary believed Gabriel. What the one destroyed by believing, the other erased by believing. Eva crediderat serpenti, Maria credidit Gabrieli; quod illa credendo deliquit, ista credendo delevit. De Carne Christi 17.

entries.title: Sub Tuum Praesidium — Greek, late 3rd century
entries.body: Beneath thy compassion we take refuge, O Mother of God: do not despise our petitions in time of trouble, but rescue us from dangers, only pure, only blessed one. Ὑπὸ τὴν σὴν εὐσπλαγχνίαν καταφεύγομεν, Θεοτόκε. Τὰς ἡμῶν ἱκεσίας μὴ παρίδῃς ἐν περιστάσει, ἀλλ᾽ ἐκ κινδύνων λύτρωσαι ἡμᾶς, μόνη ἁγνή, μόνη εὐλογημένη. P. Ryl. III 470, the oldest known Marian prayer.

entries.title: Origen of Alexandria
entries.subtitle: c. 185 - c. 254 · Greek
entries.body: (Commentary on John I.6, fragment): "No one can grasp the meaning of the Gospel of John except he who has reclined upon the breast of Jesus, and who has received from Jesus Mary to be his own mother.  Commentary on John, Preface, §6.

entries.title: St. Ephrem the Syrian
entries.subtitle: c. 306 - 373 · Syriac
entries.body: (Hymns on the Nativity 16.10, Syriac original in CSCO 186): "Just as bodies were formed from Adam without the agency of a woman, so the body of the Second Adam was formed from a virgin without the agency of a man... O only woman whose praise is great in heaven and on earth! Through thee the world has been raised; through thee the angels rejoice." English (Oratio ad Sanctissimam Dei Matrem, traditionally attributed): "After the Mediator, thou art the Mediatress of the whole world.  Hymns on the Nativity 16 (verbatim); Oratio (traditional

entries.title: St. Athanasius
entries.subtitle: c. 296 - 373 · Greek
entries.body: (Athanasian school, Homily of the Papyrus of Turin): "O noble Virgin, truly thou art greater than any other greatness. For who is thy equal in greatness, O dwelling place of God the Word? To whom among all creatures shall I compare thee, O Virgin?  Homily on the Papyrus of Turin; Athanasian school.

entries.title: St. Cyril of Jerusalem
entries.subtitle: c. 313 - 386 · Greek
entries.body: Through the holy Virgin Mary all good things have come to us. Διὰ τῆς ἁγίας Παρθένου Μαρίας πάντα τὰ ἀγαθὰ ἡμῖν προσῆλθεν. Catechetical Lectures 12.15.

entries.title: St. Gregory of Nazianzus
entries.subtitle: c. 329 - 390 · Greek
entries.body: If anyone does not believe that Holy Mary is the Mother of God, he is severed from the Godhead. Εἴ τις οὐ Θεοτόκον τὴν ἁγίαν Μαρίαν ὑπολαμβάνει, χωρὶς ἐστι τῆς θεότητος. Letter 101 (First Letter to Cledonius), PG 37, 177c.

entries.title: St. John Chrysostom
entries.subtitle: c. 347 - 407 · Greek
entries.body: (Homily on the Annunciation, attrib.): "What shall I say, what shall I speak? I am amazed at the wonder. Adam, formed of virgin earth, becomes the beginning of the human race; Mary, the virgin earth, becomes the beginning of human salvation.  Homily on the Annunciation, PG 50, 791ff. (the

entries.title: St. Cyril of Alexandria
entries.subtitle: c. 376 - 444 · Greek
entries.body: Hail Mary, Mother of God, venerable treasure of the whole universe... through whom the Trinity is glorified and adored, through whom the precious cross is hymned and venerated throughout the world, through whom heaven rejoices, through whom angels and archangels are filled with joy, through whom devils are put to flight, through whom fallen man is taken up into heaven... through whom souls are saved. Χαῖρε ἀπὸ ἡμῶν, Μαρία Θεοτόκε, σεμνὸν κειμήλιον ἁπάσης τῆς οἰκουμένης... δι᾽ ἧς Τριὰς δοξάζεται καὶ προσκυνεῖται, δι᾽ ἧς ὁ τίμιος σταυρὸς ὑμνεῖται καὶ προσκυνεῖται ἐν τῇ οἰκουμένῃ, δι᾽ ἧς οὐρανὸς ἀγάλλεται, δι᾽ ἧς ἄγγελοι καὶ ἀρχάγγελοι εὐφραίνονται, δι᾽ ἧς δαίμονες φυγαδεύονται, δι᾽ ἧς ὁ πεσὼν ἄνθρωπος εἰς οὐρανὸν ἀναλαμβάνεται... δι᾽ ἧς ψυχαὶ σώζονται. Homily 4 delivered at the Council of Ephesus, 431.

entries.title: St. Peter Chrysologus
entries.subtitle: c. 380 - c. 450 · Latin
entries.body: A virgin conceived, a virgin gave birth, a virgin remained. Salvation came through a woman, just as death came through a woman. Virgo concepit, virgo peperit, virgo permansit. Salus venit per feminam, sicut mors venit per feminam. Sermons 140, 142, 146.

entries.title: St. Ambrose of Milan
entries.subtitle: c. 339 - 397 · Latin
entries.body: Mary is the temple of God, not the God of the temple. And therefore He alone is to be adored who was working in His temple. Maria templum Dei, non Deus templi. Et ideo illa sola adoranda, qui in suo templo operabatur. De Institutione Virginis 49.

entries.title: St. Jerome
entries.subtitle: c. 347 - 420 · Latin
entries.body: Death through Eve; life through Mary. Mors per Hevam, vita per Mariam. Letter 22 to Eustochium, §21.

entries.title: St. Augustine of Hippo
entries.subtitle: 354 - 430 · Latin
entries.body: ...because she cooperated by love that the faithful might be born in the Church, who are members of that Head. ...quia cooperata est caritate, ut fideles in Ecclesia nascerentur, quae illius capitis membra sunt. De Sancta Virginitate §6.

entries.title: The Akathist Hymn
entries.subtitle: c. 6th c. · Greek
entries.body: Rejoice, heavenly ladder by which God descended; rejoice, bridge leading those of earth to heaven; rejoice, redemption of the tears of Eve. Χαῖρε, κλίμαξ ἐπουράνιε, δι᾽ ἧς κατέβη ὁ Θεός· χαῖρε, γέφυρα μετάγουσα τοὺς ἐκ γῆς πρὸς οὐρανόν· χαῖρε, λύτρωσις τῶν δακρύων τῆς Εὔας. Akathist Hymn, Stanza 2.

entries.title: St. Sophronius of Jerusalem
entries.subtitle: c. 560 - 638 · Greek
entries.body: (Sermo in Annuntiationem, PG 87, 3, 3217ff.): "No one is filled with the knowledge of God except through thee, O most holy one; no one is saved except through thee, O Mother of God; no one is ransomed from danger except through thee, O Virgin Mother.  Marian homilies of the patriarch of Jerusalem.

entries.title: St. Germanus of Constantinople
entries.subtitle: c. 634 - 733 · Greek
entries.body: (Homily on the Sash of the Theotokos, PG 98): "No one obtains the gift of mercy save through thee, O most pure; no one is saved save through thee, O most holy; no one is delivered from evils save through thee, O most chaste; no one obtains a gift of grace save through thee, O all-immaculate.  Sermons on the Mother of God, PG 98.

entries.title: St. Andrew of Crete
entries.subtitle: c. 650 - 740 · Greek
entries.body: (Homily on the Dormition II): "Today the source of life is laid in the tomb, and through her own death she becomes a ladder by which God comes down to mortals... Hail, full of grace; hail, by whom is restored mourning Eve; hail, Mediatress [Mesitria] of the world.  Homiliae, PG 97.

entries.title: St. John of Damascus
entries.subtitle: c. 675 - 749 · Greek
entries.body: Thou art the wonder of all wonders, the saving Mediatress of the world. Σὺ θαῦμα τῶν θαυμάτων, ἡ σώζουσα τὸν κόσμον μεσῖτις. Homilies on the Dormition I-III, PG 96.

entries.title: St. Peter Damian
entries.subtitle: 1007 - 1072 · Latin
entries.body: Nothing is impossible for thee, who canst even raise those who are in despair to the hope of salvation. Tibi nihil impossibile, cui possibile est etiam desperatos in spem salutis revocare. Sermons on the Nativity of Mary.

entries.title: St. Anselm of Canterbury
entries.subtitle: 1033 - 1109 · Latin
entries.body: Nothing equals Mary; nothing but God is greater than Mary. Nihil Mariae aequale, nihil nisi Deus maius Maria. Oratio 52, Prayer to the Virgin.

entries.title: St. Bernard of Clairvaux
entries.subtitle: 1090 - 1153 · Latin, "Marian Doctor"
entries.body: He willed that we should have everything through Mary. Totum nos habere voluit per Mariam. Sermon on the Aqueduct (In Nativitate B. Mariae), §7.

entries.title: St. Albert the Great
entries.subtitle: c. 1206 - 1280 · Latin
entries.body: The Blessed Virgin was chosen by God not to be the minister of some particular work, but to be the cooperatrix and helper [cooperatrix et adiutrix] of His whole plan. Beata Virgo electa est a Deo non in ministram alicuius particularis, sed in cooperatricem et adiutricem totius eius negotii. Mariale super Missus est, q. 42 (Albertine school).

entries.title: St. Bonaventure
entries.subtitle: 1221 - 1274 · Latin, "Seraphic Doctor"
entries.body: As the moon, set between the sun and the earth, communicates to the earth what it receives from the sun, so Mary, set between Christ and us, pours out upon us the graces she receives from Christ. Sicut luna inter solem et terram interposita, quod a sole accipit, terrae communicat, sic Maria inter Christum et nos posita, gratias quas a Christo accipit, nobis effundit. Speculum B.V.M. ch. 6 (Bonaventurian school).

entries.title: St. Thomas Aquinas
entries.subtitle: 1225 - 1274 · Latin, "Angelic Doctor"
entries.body: ...the consent of the Virgin in the place of the whole human nature was awaited [at the Annunciation]. ...expectabatur consensus Virginis loco totius humanae naturae. Summa Theologiae III, q. 30, a. 1, c.

entries.title: St. Bridget of Sweden
entries.subtitle: 1303 - 1373 · Latin (the Revelations
entries.body: My Mother and I have saved man as it were with one Heart only: I by suffering in My Heart and flesh, she by the sorrow and love of her heart. Mater mea et ego salvavimus hominem quasi uno corde tantum, ego patiendo in corde et carne, illa dolore et amore cordis sui. Revelations I.35 (private revelation; received in the

entries.title: Jean Gerson
entries.subtitle: 1363 - 1429 · Latin
entries.body: Mary, our Co-Redemptrix. Maria coredemptrix nostra. Marian writings (the precise textual provenance of

entries.title: St. Bernardine of Siena
entries.subtitle: 1380 - 1444 · Latin
entries.body: Every grace communicated to this world has a threefold motion: for, by a most ordered dispensation, it is given from God to Christ, from Christ to the Virgin, from the Virgin to us. Omnis gratia quae communicatur huic mundo triplicem habet processum: nam ordinatissime a Deo in Christum, a Christo in Virginem, a Virgine in nos dispensatur. Sermo 51, in Opera Omnia, Quaracchi, vol. II.

entries.title: St. Peter Canisius
entries.subtitle: 1521 - 1597 · Latin
entries.body: The Most Blessed Virgin is so united with Christ our Redeemer that she must most truly be called a cooperatrix in the work of redemption. Beatissima Virgo cum Christo Redemptore nostro adeo coniuncta est, ut in opere redemptionis verissime cooperatrix sit dicenda. *De Maria Virgine Incomparabili et Dei Genitrice

entries.title: St. Robert Bellarmine
entries.subtitle: 1542 - 1621 · Latin
entries.body: Mary offered her Son in sacrifice, afflicted with such sufferings together with Him as no other creature ever bore. Maria... Filium suum in sacrificium obtulit, tantis doloribus cum eo afflicta, quantis nulla unquam creatura. De Septem Verbis Christi in Cruce, Book I, ch. 12.

entries.title: St. Francis de Sales
entries.subtitle: 1567 - 1622 · French (Latin in
entries.body: Mary is Mediatrix of intercession between us and Jesus Christ, just as Jesus Christ is Mediator of redemption between us and God His Father. Marie est Médiatrice d'intercession entre nous et Jésus-Christ, comme Jésus-Christ est Médiateur de rédemption entre nous et Dieu son Père. Treatise on the Love of God III.8 (substance); Letters.

entries.title: St. John Eudes
entries.subtitle: 1601 - 1680 · French
entries.body: (The Admirable Heart of Mary, Book III): "The Heart of Mary is so completely united to the Heart of Jesus, and so transformed into it, that they are no longer two hearts but one single Heart.  Le Cœur Admirable de la Très Sacrée Mère de Dieu, Book III.

entries.title: St. Louis Marie de Montfort
entries.subtitle: 1673 - 1716 · French
entries.body: God the Father gathered all the waters together and called them the seas (maria); He gathered all His graces together and called them Mary (Maria). Dieu le Père a fait un amas de toutes les eaux qu'il a appelé la mer (*maria*) ; il a fait un amas de toutes ses grâces qu'il a appelé **Marie**. True Devotion to Mary, §23.

entries.title: St. Alphonsus Liguori
entries.subtitle: 1696 - 1787 · Italian
entries.body: All graces are dispensed to us through Mary, and all those who are saved are saved only by means of this Divine Mother. Tutte le grazie ci vengono dispensate per mezzo di Maria, e tutti quelli che si salvano si salvano solo per mezzo di questa divina Madre. Le Glorie di Maria, Part I, ch. 5.

entries.title: St. Catherine Labouré
entries.subtitle: 1806 - 1876 · Miraculous Medal inscription, 1830 — French
entries.body: O Mary, conceived without sin, pray for us who have recourse to thee. Ô Marie, conçue sans péché, priez pour nous qui avons recours à vous. Apparition at Rue du Bac, 27 November 1830.

entries.title: St. Bernadette Soubirous
entries.subtitle: 1844 - 1879 · Occitan, 1858
entries.body: I am the Immaculate Conception. Que soy era Immaculada Councepciou. Sixteenth apparition at Lourdes, on the feast of the

entries.title: St. Maximilian Maria Kolbe
entries.subtitle: 1894 - 1941 · Polish
entries.body: The Immaculata is the Mediatrix of all graces. Niepokalana jest Pośredniczką wszelkich łask. Pisma (Writings), conferences, and the Militia Immaculatae

entries.title: St. (Padre) Pio of Pietrelcina
entries.subtitle: 1887 - 1968 · Italian
entries.body: Love the Madonna and make her loved. Always pray the Rosary. Amate la Madonna e fatela amare. Recitate sempre il Rosario. Epistolario and pastoral counsel, passim.

entries.title: Bl. Fulton J. Sheen
entries.subtitle: 1895 - 1979 · English
entries.body: When a woman gives birth, she does so with much pain; Mary in giving birth at Bethlehem had no pain; she had her pain at Calvary, when the Mystical Body of her Son was born. The World's First Love, ch. 12.

entries.title: St. Teresa of Calcutta
entries.subtitle: 1910 - 1997 · English
entries.body: Mary, Mother of Jesus, please be a mother to me now. Mother Teresa: In My Own Words and conferences.

entries.title: Bl. Pope Pius IX
entries.subtitle: 1792 - 1878 · Latin
entries.body: She, in her wholly singular way, crushed the poisonous head of the serpent and brought salvation to the world. Ipsa enim eximio prorsus modo venenosi serpentis caput contrivit, et salutem mundo attulit. Ineffabilis Deus, apostolic constitution defining the

entries.title: Pope Leo XIII
entries.subtitle: 1810 - 1903 · Latin
entries.body: Mary... is the channel of every grace and gift. ...Maria... omnis gratiae et doni canalis. Fidentem Piumque Animum (1896).

entries.title: Pope St. Pius X
entries.subtitle: 1835 - 1914 · Latin
entries.body: We may with truth and propriety call her the Reparatrix of the lost world, and therefore the Dispensatrix of all the gifts that Jesus has acquired for us by His death and by His Blood. ...merito ipsam reparatricem perditi orbis et dispensatricem omnium munerum, quae nobis Iesus nece et sanguine comparavit, appellare possumus. Ad Diem Illum Laetissimum (1904).

entries.title: Pope Benedict XV
entries.subtitle: 1854 - 1922 · Latin (the high-water mark)
entries.body: To such an extent did she suffer and almost die with her suffering and dying Son; to such an extent did she surrender her maternal rights over her Son for man's salvation, and immolated Him, insofar as she could, to appease the justice of God, that we may rightly say she redeemed the human race together with Christ. Ita cum Filio patiente et moriente passa est et paene commortua; sic materna in Filium iura pro hominum salute abdicavit, placandaeque Dei iustitiae, quantum ad se pertinebat, Filium immolavit, ut merito dici queat eam **cum Christo humanum genus redemisse**. Inter Sodalicia (1918), AAS 10:181-182.

entries.title: Pope Pius XI
entries.subtitle: 1857 - 1939 · Italian / Latin
entries.body: O Mother of love and mercy, who, when thy sweetest Son was consummating the Redemption of the human race on the altar of the Cross, didst stand next to Him, suffering with Him as Coredemptrix... O Madre di amore e di misericordia, che quando il tuo dolcissimo Figlio consumava la Redenzione del genere umano sull'ara della croce, sei stata a Lui vicina, soffrendo con Lui come Corredentrice... Radio message to Lourdes, 28 April 1935. AAS 27 (1935) 158.

entries.title: Ven. Pope Pius XII
entries.subtitle: 1876 - 1958 · Latin
entries.body: She it was who, immune from all sin, personal or inherited, and ever most closely united with her Son, offered Him on Golgotha to the Eternal Father, together with the holocaust of her maternal rights and motherly love, like a new Eve, for all the children of Adam. Ipsa quae, ab omni labe, sive personali, sive hereditaria, immunis et arctissime semper cum Filio suo coniuncta, eumdem in Golgotha, una cum maternorum iurium maternique amoris holocausto, nova veluti Eva, pro omnibus Adae filiis... aeterno Patri obtulit. Mystici Corporis Christi (1943).

entries.title: Second Vatican Council, Lumen Gentium §62
entries.subtitle: 21 November 1964 · Latin
entries.body: Therefore the Blessed Virgin is invoked in the Church under the titles of Advocate, Helper, Benefactress, and Mediatrix. This, however, is so understood that it neither takes away anything from nor adds anything to the dignity and efficacy of Christ the one Mediator. Quapropter Beata Virgo in Ecclesia titulis Advocatae, Auxiliatricis, Adiutricis, **Mediatricis** invocatur. Quod tamen ita intelligitur, ut dignitati et efficacitati Christi unius Mediatoris nihil deroget, nihil superaddat. Lumen Gentium ch. VIII, §62.

entries.title: Pope St. John Paul II
entries.subtitle: 1920 - 2005 · Italian
entries.body: Mary, though conceived and born without the taint of original sin, participated in a marvelous way in the sufferings of her divine Son, in order to be Coredemptrix of humanity. Maria, sebbene concepita e nata immune dalla colpa originale, ha partecipato in maniera mirabile alle sofferenze del suo Figlio divino, per essere **Corredentrice** dell'umanità. General Audience of 8 September 1982; the title

entries.title: St. Epiphanius of Salamis
entries.subtitle: c. 310 – 403 · Greek
entries.body: Let Mary be held in honour; the Lord alone be adored. Ἐν τιμῇ Μαρία, ὁ Κύριος προσκυνείσθω. Panarion (Refutation of All Heresies) 79, against the

entries.title: St. Bede the Venerable
entries.subtitle: 672 – 735 · Latin
entries.body: Blessed Mary, by believing, not only entered into the hidden mystery of the Father, but received the Word in her flesh and, with that flesh, the fullness of the Godhead. Beata Maria... non solum credendo intra Patris arcanum sacramenta cognovit, sed in carne suscepit Verbum, et cum carne, ut ait Apostolus, plenitudinem deitatis. Homiliae Evangelii I.4, on the Visitation (PL 94, 12;

entries.title: Bl. Amadeus of Lausanne
entries.subtitle: 1110 – 1159 · Latin
entries.body: Rising dawn, chosen as the sun, beautiful as the moon, terrible as an army set in battle array. Aurora consurgens, electa ut sol, pulchra ut luna, terribilis ut castrorum acies ordinata. Eight Homilies on the Praise of Mary (Sources

entries.title: Bl. John Duns Scotus
entries.subtitle: c. 1266 – 1308 · Latin, "Subtle Doctor"
entries.body: He could; it was fitting; therefore he did. Potuit, decuit, ergo fecit. Ordinatio III, d.3, q.1, Vatican Critical Edition vol.

entries.title: St. Lawrence Justinian
entries.subtitle: 1381 – 1456 · Latin
entries.body: (Sermo de Annuntiatione, 15th-century Patriarch of Venice; substance): "The Holy Spirit, like a great river, flowed into the Heart of Mary, and from the Heart of Mary flows back into us. She is the channel by which graces are dispensed, the maternal aqueduct of the divine mercy.  Sermo de Annuntiatione and *De Casto Connubio Verbi

entries.title: St. Teresa of Ávila
entries.subtitle: 1515 – 1582 · Spanish, Doctor of the Church
entries.body: I took the glorious Virgin for my Lady and Advocate and entrusted myself wholly to her. Tomé por mi señora y abogada a la gloriosa Virgen, y me encomendé a ella muy de veras. Libro de la Vida (Autobiography), ch. 1, on her

entries.title: Bl. John Henry Newman
entries.subtitle: 1801 – 1890 · English, Cardinal, "Doctor of Marian Theology" (informal)
entries.body: (Letter to Pusey on Occasion of His Eirenicon, 1866; the famous Marian passage): "Mary is the daughter of Sion exalted, the lady to whom all the prophets minister, who is, after the Eternal Word, the most exalted of beings.  Letter to Dr. Pusey on Occasion of His Eirenicon

entries.title: The Protoevangelium
entries.subtitle: Genesis 3:15
entries.body: > Hebrew (Masoretic Text): *ואיבה אשית בינך ובין האשה ובין זרעך ובין זרעה הוא ישופך ראש ואתה תשופנו עקב* "And I will put enmities between thee and the woman, and thy seed and her seed: she [Vulgate: *ipsa*] shall crush thy head, and thou shalt lie in wait for her heel." (Genesis 3:15, Douay-Rheims)

entries.title: The First Eve (Type by Inverse)
entries.subtitle: Genesis 2-3
entries.body: Eve is the type by inverse. She is what Mary undoes by doing the opposite. The parallels are exact: "And Adam called his wife's name Eve [*Hawwah*, 'living']; because she was the mother of all the living." (Genesis 3:20, Douay)

entries.title: Rebekah, Mediatrix of the Blessing
entries.subtitle: Genesis 24:67 and 27
entries.body: > "And Isaac brought her into his mother Sara's tent, and took her to wife: and he loved her so much, that it moderated the sorrow which was occasioned by his mother's death." (Genesis 24:67) "And Rebecca said to Jacob her son: I heard thy father speaking with Esau thy brother... Now therefore, my son, follow my counsel..." (Genesis 27:6, 8)

entries.title: Jacob's Ladder
entries.subtitle: Genesis 28:10-22
entries.body: Jacob's ladder is one of the most universal Marian types, found in every era of the tradition. "And he saw in a dream a ladder standing upon the earth, and the top thereof touching heaven: the angels also of God ascending and descending by it... This is no other but the house of God, and the gate of heaven." (Genesis 28:12, 17)

entries.title: The Burning Bush
entries.subtitle: Exodus 3:1-6
entries.body: The bush burns yet is not consumed. The presence of God dwells in created matter without destroying it. The type is exact for the Theotokos. "And the Lord appeared to him in a flame of fire out of the midst of a bush: and he saw that the bush was on fire, and was not burnt." (Exodus 3:2)

entries.title: The Pillar of Cloud
entries.subtitle: Exodus 13:21-22
entries.body: The pillar of cloud and fire is the visible sign of God's presence guiding Israel through the wilderness. The cloud, in particular, is identified by the Fathers with the shekinah (the divine indwelling glory). "And the Lord went before them to shew the way by day in a pillar of a cloud, and by night in a pillar of fire: that he might be the guide of their journey at both times." (Exodus 13:21)

entries.title: Miriam the Prophetess
entries.subtitle: Exodus 15:20-21
entries.body: Miriam is a type by name and by function. "So Mary [Miriam] the prophetess, the sister of Aaron, took a timbrel in her hand: and all the women went forth after her with timbrels and with dances: and she began the song to them, saying: Let us sing to the Lord, for he is gloriously magnified: the horse and his rider he hath thrown into the sea." (Exodus 15:20-21)

entries.title: The Ark of the Covenant and the Mercy Seat
entries.subtitle: Exodus 25:10-22
entries.body: The Ark of the Covenant is the densest and most extensively developed Marian type in the patristic tradition. Eight elements concur. "And they shall make an ark of setim wood... and thou shalt make a propitiatory of the purest gold... and thence will I give orders, and will speak to thee over the propitiatory, and from the midst of the two cherubim, which shall be upon the ark of the testimony, all things which I will command the children of Israel by thee." (Exodus 25:10, 17, 22)

entries.title: Aaron's Rod That Budded
entries.subtitle: Numbers 17
entries.body: The dead rod of Aaron, placed in the tabernacle, miraculously buds and brings forth almonds without natural cause. The miraculous fruitfulness of what nature could not produce. "He found that the rod of Aaron, for the house of Levi, was budded, and that the buds swelling it had bloomed blossoms, which spreading the leaves, were formed into almonds." (Numbers 17:8)

entries.title: Deborah the Judge and Prophetess
entries.subtitle: Judges 4-5
entries.body: Deborah is a woman through whom God delivers Israel from a foreign oppressor. She is a judge (the only woman judge in Israel), a prophetess, and the strategic mind behind the victory. The Song of Deborah (Judges 5) is the second great song of Israel after the Song of Miriam. "Now Debbora, a prophetess, the wife of Lapidoth, judged the people at that time... And she said to him: Surely thou shalt go with me, but at this time the victory shall not be attributed to thee, because Sisara shall be delivered into the hand of a woman." (Judges 4:4, 9)

entries.title: Jael the Tent-Peg
entries.subtitle: Judges 4:17-22
entries.body: Jael, a woman, crushes the head of Sisara, the tyrant general of Israel's oppressor. The verbal parallel to Genesis 3:15 is too close to miss: a woman, crushing the head of the enemy. "And she went out to meet him, and said to him: Come in to me, my lord, come in, fear not... She took a nail of the tent, and taking also a hammer: and going in softly, and with silence, she put the nail upon the temples of his head, and striking it with the hammer, drove it through his brain fast into the ground." (Judges 4:18, 21)

entries.title: Gideon's Fleece
entries.subtitle: Judges 6:36-40
entries.body: Two miracles, in succession: dew on the fleece while the ground is dry, then the ground wet with dew while the fleece remains dry. "If thou wilt save Israel by my hand, as thou hast said, behold I will put this fleece of wool on the floor: if there be dew in the fleece only, and it be dry on all the ground beside, I shall know that by my hand, as thou hast said, thou wilt deliver Israel." (Judges 6:36-37)

entries.title: Hannah and Her Magnificat-Prefigurement
entries.subtitle: 1 Samuel 1-2
entries.body: Hannah's prayer in 1 Samuel 2 is the direct Old Testament template for Mary's Magnificat in Luke 1:46-55. The parallels are precise: "Hannah prayed, and said: My heart hath rejoiced in the Lord, and my horn is exalted in my God: my mouth is enlarged over my enemies: because I have joyed in thy salvation... He hath put down the mighty from their seat, and hath exalted the humble." (1 Samuel 2:1, 7-8)

entries.title: Bathsheba and the Davidic Queen Mother (Gebirah)
entries.subtitle: 1 Kings 2:19-20
entries.body: This is the single sharpest Mediatrix type in the entire Old Testament. The structural parallel is exact and detailed. "And Bathsheba came to king Solomon, to speak to him for Adonias: and the king arose to meet her, and bowed to her, and sat down upon his throne: and a throne was set for the king's mother, and she sat on his right hand. Then she said to him: I desire one small petition of thee; deny me not. And the king said to her: My mother, ask: for I must not turn away thy face." (1 Kings 2:19-20)

entries.title: The Ark Comes to Jerusalem
entries.subtitle: 2 Samuel 6
entries.body: See §8 above (the Ark of the Covenant). 2 Samuel 6 is the focal Old Testament text for the Marian Ark typology because of the verbal parallels with Luke 1:39-56.

entries.title: Wisdom Personified
entries.subtitle: Proverbs 8
entries.body: Wisdom in Proverbs 8 speaks in feminine voice and is personified as a preexistent figure present with God at creation. The principal Catholic reading of this passage is Christological: Wisdom is the Eternal Logos, the Word. This is the universal patristic understanding (Athanasius, Basil, Hilary all defend this against Arianism, which read Proverbs 8:22 as a proof that the Son was a creature). "The Lord possessed me in the beginning of his ways, before he made any thing from the beginning. I was set up from eternity, and of old before the earth was made... When he prepared the heavens, I was present... I was with him forming all things." (Proverbs 8:22-23, 27, 30)

entries.title: The Valiant Woman (Mulier Fortis)
entries.subtitle: Proverbs 31:10-31
entries.body: The closing acrostic poem of Proverbs is read by the tradition both as a description of the ideal wife and, in sensus plenior, as a portrait of: "Who shall find a valiant woman? far and from the uttermost coasts is the price of her... Strength and beauty are her clothing, and she shall laugh in the latter day... Many daughters have gathered together riches: thou hast surpassed them all... Give her of the fruit of her hands: and let her works praise her in the gates." (Proverbs 31:10, 25, 29, 31)

entries.title: The Bride, the Enclosed Garden, the Closed Spring
entries.subtitle: Song of Songs
entries.body: The Song of Songs is read by the Fathers and Doctors on three concentric levels: "Thou art all fair, O my love, and there is no spot in thee... A garden enclosed [*hortus conclusus*] is my sister, my spouse, a garden enclosed, a fountain sealed up." (Song of Songs 4:7, 4:12)

entries.title: Wisdom of Solomon 7-9, Sirach 24
entries.body: The deuterocanonical Wisdom literature continues and intensifies the personification of Wisdom from Proverbs 8. Sirach 24, in particular, has Wisdom speak in feminine voice and describe herself as universally fruitful, the source of every virtue, the inheritance of those who love her. "I am the mother of fair love, and of fear, and of knowledge, and of holy hope... Come over to me, all ye that desire me, and be filled with my fruits. For my spirit is sweet above honey, and my inheritance above honey and the honeycomb." (Sirach 24:24, 26-27)

entries.title: The Virgin Shall Conceive
entries.subtitle: Isaiah 7:14
entries.body: The most directly Marian text in the Old Testament after Genesis 3:15. The LXX parthenos (virgin) is preserved in Matthew 1:23 as the fulfillment text for the Annunciation. The dogmatic content (virginal conception) is more central than the Marian title. "Therefore the Lord himself shall give you a sign. Behold a virgin [*almah*; LXX *parthenos*] shall conceive, and bear a son, and his name shall be called Emmanuel." (Isaiah 7:14)

entries.title: The Closed Gate
entries.subtitle: Ezekiel 44:1-3
entries.body: The closed eastern gate of the visionary temple. Through it the Lord enters; through it none other passes; it remains closed. "And he brought me back to the way of the gate of the outward sanctuary, which looked towards the east: and it was shut. And the Lord said to me: This gate shall be shut; it shall not be opened, and no man shall pass through it: because the Lord the God of Israel hath entered in by it, and it shall be shut for the prince." (Ezekiel 44:1-3)

entries.title: Daughter of Sion, the Lord in Thy Midst
entries.subtitle: Zephaniah 3:14-17
entries.body: The Septuagint of Zephaniah 3:14 reads chaire sphodra, thugater Sion (Rejoice greatly, daughter of Sion). The angel Gabriel's greeting to Mary in Luke 1:28 is chaire kecharitomene. The verbal echo is deliberate: Mary is hailed as the Daughter of Sion personified, the place where God has come to dwell in the midst of His people. "Sing, O daughter of Sion: shout, O Israel: be glad and rejoice with all the heart, O daughter of Jerusalem... The Lord thy God is in the midst of thee, he is mighty, he will save: he will rejoice over thee with gladness, he will be silent in his love, he will be joyful over thee in praise." (Zephaniah 3:14, 17)

entries.title: Rejoice Greatly, O Daughter of Sion
entries.subtitle: Zechariah 9:9
entries.body: The same chaire greeting addressed to the Daughter of Sion, this time in the context of the coming of the messianic King. The verse is the direct Old Testament source for the Palm Sunday entry of Christ into Jerusalem (Matthew 21:5, John 12:15), but the chaire thugater Sion formula is the Old Testament root of the Lucan Annunciation greeting. "Rejoice greatly, O daughter of Sion, shout for joy, O daughter of Jerusalem: BEHOLD THY KING will come to thee, the just and saviour: he is poor, and riding upon an ass, and upon a colt the foal of an ass." (Zechariah 9:9)

entries.title: The Stone Cut Out Without Hands
entries.subtitle: Daniel 2:34-35, 45
entries.body: The mountain from which the stone is cut without human agency is read by the Fathers as a type of Mary. The stone is Christ, cut from her without the action of man (the virginal conception). The mountain (Mary) is distinguished from the stone (Christ) but inseparable from it. "Thus thou sawest, till a stone was cut out of a mountain without hands: and it struck the statue upon the feet thereof that were of iron and of clay, and broke them in pieces... but the stone that struck the statue, became a great mountain, and filled the whole earth." (Daniel 2:34-35)

entries.title: The Queen Who Risks Her Life to Intercede
entries.subtitle: Esther
entries.body: Esther, raised from among her condemned people to royal dignity, risks her life by entering the king's presence uninvited, to intercede for the salvation of her people. The king extends the golden sceptre; the intercession is accepted; the people are delivered. "All the king's servants, and all the provinces that are under his dominion, know, that whosoever, whether man or woman, cometh into the king's inner court, who is not called for, is immediately to be put to death without any delay: except the king shall hold out the golden sceptre to him... Now therefore, who knoweth whether thou art not therefore come to the kingdom, that thou mightest be ready in such a time as this?" (Esther 4:11, 14)

entries.title: The Woman Who Crushed the Tyrant's Head
entries.subtitle: Judith
entries.body: Judith, alone, by her beauty, prudence, and faith, infiltrates the camp of Holofernes, the Assyrian general besieging Bethulia, and severs his head. The people are delivered without the loss of a single life. "And she struck twice upon his neck, and cut off his head, and took off his canopy from the pillars, and rolled away his headless body... Then Ozias the prince of the people of Israel, said to her: Blessed art thou, O daughter, by the Lord the most high God, above all women upon the earth." (Judith 13:10, 23)

entries.title: Stanza 1 (Kontakion 1, alpha)
entries.body: "An angel of highest rank was sent from heaven to say to the Mother of God: Rejoice! And, beholding thee, O Lord, taking bodily form, he was filled with awe and stood crying out to her with his bodiless voice such things as these:"

entries.title: Stanza 2 (Ikos 1, beta)
entries.subtitle: The First Set of Salutations
entries.body: 1. Rejoice, through whom joy shall shine forth. 2. Rejoice, through whom the curse shall vanish. 3. Rejoice, recalling of fallen Adam. 4. Rejoice, redemption of the tears of Eve. 5. Rejoice, height untrod by human thoughts. 6. Rejoice, depth unseen even by angelic eyes. 7. Rejoice, for thou art the throne of the King. 8. Rejoice, for thou holdest him who upholds all. 9. Rejoice, star which heralds the Sun. 10. Rejoice, womb of the divine Incarnation. 11. Rejoice, through whom creation is renewed. 12. Rejoice, through whom the Creator becomes a babe. **Refrain**: *Chaire, Nymphe Anympheute*. (Rejoice, O Bride Unwedded.) λύτρωσις τῶν δακρύων τῆς Εὔας

entries.title: Stanza 3 (Kontakion 2, gamma)
entries.body: "The holy Virgin, seeing herself most pure, said to Gabriel: How shall the marvel of thy strange utterance be possible? For thou sayest that I shall conceive in my womb without seed. Whence then? And he, awe-stricken, cried out: Alleluia!"

entries.title: Stanza 4 (Ikos 2, delta)
entries.body: 1. Rejoice, mystery hidden from the ages. 2. Rejoice, faith of those who pray in silence. 3. Rejoice, preamble of the miracles of Christ. 4. Rejoice, fountain of his commandments. 5. Rejoice, heavenly ladder by which God came down. 6. Rejoice, bridge leading those of earth to heaven. 7. Rejoice, much-marveled wonder of the angels. 8. Rejoice, much-bewailed sorrow of demons. 9. Rejoice, who didst ineffably give birth to the Light. 10. Rejoice, who didst initiate no one into the manner. 11. Rejoice, who dost surpass the wisdom of the wise. 12. Rejoice, who dost enlighten the minds of the faithful.

entries.title: Stanza 5 (Kontakion 3, epsilon)
entries.body: "Then a power of the Most High overshadowed the Virgin, that she might conceive, and her fruitful womb was made a pleasing field for all those who would reap salvation, when they sing: Alleluia!" ἐπεσκίασεν

entries.title: Stanza 6 (Ikos 3, zeta)
entries.subtitle: The Visitation
entries.body: 1. Rejoice, branch of the unfading shoot. 2. Rejoice, dwelling-place of the immortal fruit. 3. Rejoice, who dost cultivate the Cultivator of mankind. 4. Rejoice, who dost give growth to the Sower of our life. 5. Rejoice, field producing the abundant compassion of God. 6. Rejoice, table bearing the abundance of mercy. 7. Rejoice, who dost cause the meadow of delight to flourish. 8. Rejoice, who dost prepare a haven for souls. 9. Rejoice, acceptable incense of intercession. 10. Rejoice, propitiation of the whole world. 11. Rejoice, kindness of God to mortal man. 12. Rejoice, confidence of mortal man before God. ἱλασμὸς τοῦ παντὸς κόσμου

entries.title: Stanza 7 (Kontakion 4, eta)
entries.body: "Bearing within himself a tempest of doubting thoughts, the chaste Joseph was perturbed... He learned of thy seedless conception, O spotless one, and cried out: Alleluia!"

entries.title: Stanza 8 (Ikos 4, theta)
entries.subtitle: The Shepherds and Bethlehem
entries.body: 1. Rejoice, Mother of the Lamb and the Shepherd. 2. Rejoice, sheepfold of rational sheep. 3. Rejoice, defense against unseen enemies. 4. Rejoice, opening of the gates of paradise. 5. Rejoice, for the things of heaven rejoice with earth. 6. Rejoice, for the things of earth dance with heaven. 7. Rejoice, never-silent voice of the apostles. 8. Rejoice, never-defeated courage of the champions. 9. Rejoice, firm support of the faith. 10. Rejoice, shining herald of grace. 11. Rejoice, through whom Hades was stripped bare. 12. Rejoice, through whom we were clothed with glory.

entries.title: Stanza 9 (Kontakion 5, iota)
entries.subtitle: The Magi
entries.body: "Gazing upon the divine star, the Magi followed its brilliance... and beholding Christ, they cried out to him: Alleluia!"

entries.title: Stanza 10 (Ikos 5, kappa)
entries.subtitle: The Magi at Bethlehem
entries.body: 1. Rejoice, Mother of the star which never sets. 2. Rejoice, dawn of the mystical day. 3. Rejoice, who didst quench the furnace of error. 4. Rejoice, who dost illumine the initiates of the Trinity. 5. Rejoice, who didst cast out from authority the inhuman tyrant. 6. Rejoice, who didst show forth Christ the Lord, the Lover of    mankind. 7. Rejoice, deliverer from barbarian worship. 8. Rejoice, who dost rescue from the mire of works. 9. Rejoice, who didst quench the worship of fire. 10. Rejoice, who dost deliver from the flame of passions. 11. Rejoice, guide of the faithful in chastity. 12. Rejoice, joy of all generations.

entries.title: Stanza 11 (Kontakion 6, lambda)
entries.body: "Having become heralds bearing God, the Magi turned back to Babylon... and Babylon they left empty... so we, having beheld Christ, cry out: Alleluia!"

entries.title: Stanza 12 (Ikos 6, mu)
entries.subtitle: The Magi as Apostles
entries.body: 1. Rejoice, raising up of fallen Adam. 2. Rejoice, redemption of the tears of Eve. 3. Rejoice, height too hard to climb for human thoughts. 4. Rejoice, depth too deep to fathom even for angelic eyes. 5. Rejoice, throne of the King. 6. Rejoice, for thou bearest him who bears all things. 7. Rejoice, star which heralds the Sun. 8. Rejoice, womb of the divine Incarnation. 9. Rejoice, through whom creation is renewed. 10. Rejoice, through whom the Creator becomes a babe. 11. Rejoice, mountain of the divine glory. 12. Rejoice, deeply-rooted lily of immortality.

entries.title: Stanza 13 (Kontakion 7, nu)
entries.body: "When Simeon was about to depart this present age, thou wast given to him as a babe... and he sang to him: Alleluia!"

entries.title: Stanza 14 (Ikos 7, xi)
entries.subtitle: Simeon and the Presentation
entries.body: 1. Rejoice, all-immaculate Bride. 2. Rejoice, sacred chariot of him who sits upon the Cherubim. 3. Rejoice, glorious dwelling of him who sits upon the Seraphim. 4. Rejoice, who didst unite opposites. 5. Rejoice, who didst combine virginity and child-bearing. 6. Rejoice, through whom transgression has been undone. 7. Rejoice, through whom paradise has been opened. 8. Rejoice, key of the kingdom of Christ. 9. Rejoice, hope of eternal good things. 10. Rejoice, sweet-smelling fragrance of Christ. 11. Rejoice, life of mystical festivity. 12. Rejoice, who didst undo the laws of nature.

entries.title: Stanza 15 (Kontakion 8, omicron)
entries.body: "A strange birth was beheld... Let us withdraw our minds from earthly things, transporting them to heaven. For for this the highest God appeared on earth as a humble man, in order to draw upward those who cry to him: Alleluia!"

entries.title: Stanza 16 (Ikos 8, pi)
entries.subtitle: The Cosmic Christ
entries.body: 1. Rejoice, who didst contain the uncontainable God. 2. Rejoice, gate of the venerable mystery. 3. Rejoice, doubtful hearing of the unfaithful. 4. Rejoice, certain glory of the faithful. 5. Rejoice, all-holy chariot of him who is above the Cherubim. 6. Rejoice, perfect dwelling of him who is above the Seraphim. 7. Rejoice, who didst bring opposites into harmony. 8. Rejoice, who didst join virginity and motherhood. 9. Rejoice, through whom transgression was annulled. 10. Rejoice, through whom paradise was opened. 11. Rejoice, key of the kingdom of Christ. 12. Rejoice, hope of good things eternal. χωρίον τοῦ ἀχωρήτου Θεοῦ

entries.title: Stanza 17 (Kontakion 9, rho)
entries.body: "All angelic nature marveled at the great work of thy Incarnation, for they saw the inapproachable God approachable to all... O Lover of mankind, Alleluia!"

entries.title: Stanza 18 (Ikos 9, sigma)
entries.subtitle: The Mystery of the Word
entries.body: 1. Rejoice, container of God which has no bounds. 2. Rejoice, gate of the venerable mystery. 3. Rejoice, doubtful report to unbelievers. 4. Rejoice, undoubted boast of the faithful. 5. Rejoice, all-holy chariot of him who is above the Cherubim. 6. Rejoice, glorious dwelling of him who is above the Seraphim. 7. Rejoice, who didst bring opposites to the same end. 8. Rejoice, who didst combine virginity and child-bearing. 9. Rejoice, through whom transgression was loosed. 10. Rejoice, through whom paradise was opened. 11. Rejoice, key of the kingdom of Christ. 12. Rejoice, hope of eternal good things.

entries.title: Stanza 19 (Kontakion 10, tau)
entries.body: "Seeking to save the world, the Creator of all came to it of his own will... and remained what he was... wherefore we sing: Alleluia!"

entries.title: Stanza 20 (Ikos 10, upsilon)
entries.subtitle: The Cosmic Mediatrix
entries.body: 1. Rejoice, refuge of virgins. 2. Rejoice, dwelling of the faithful. 3. Rejoice, who didst exalt the cherubim. 4. Rejoice, who didst shine on the archangels. 5. Rejoice, fall of the demons. 6. Rejoice, light from a single ray. 7. Rejoice, who dost teach the multitude of mortals. 8. Rejoice, who dost confound the dreaming of the disbelievers. 9. Rejoice, all-honored crown of chaste priests. 10. Rejoice, divinely-flowing fountain of grace. 11. Rejoice, who didst destroy the citadel of the enemy. 12. Rejoice, who didst point the way to the kingdom of God. πηγὴ θεορρύτου χάριτος

entries.title: Stanza 21 (Kontakion 11, phi)
entries.body: "Every hymn is overcome which seeks to match the multitude of thy compassions... wherefore even Cherubim cry out to thee: Alleluia!"

entries.title: Stanza 22 (Ikos 11, chi)
entries.subtitle: The Akathist Hymn Recognizes Its Own Inadequacy
entries.body: 1. Rejoice, lamp of the noetic light. 2. Rejoice, beam of the spiritual sun. 3. Rejoice, lightning bolt that illumines souls. 4. Rejoice, fearful thunder against enemies. 5. Rejoice, dawn that gives bright illumination. 6. Rejoice, full-flowing river of well-being. 7. Rejoice, who dost design the form of the baptismal font. 8. Rejoice, who dost take away the stain of sin. 9. Rejoice, washing of the conscience. 10. Rejoice, cup that draws forth joy. 11. Rejoice, fragrance of the sweetness of Christ. 12. Rejoice, life of the mystical banquet.

entries.title: Stanza 23 (Kontakion 12, psi)
entries.body: "Wishing to grant pardon for ancient debts, the One who pardons all mankind came of his own will to those who had fallen from his grace... and tearing up the document of debt, he heard from all: Alleluia!"

entries.title: Stanza 24 (Ikos 12, omega)
entries.subtitle: The Final Salutations and Conclusion
entries.body: 1. Rejoice, tabernacle of God the Word. 2. Rejoice, holy of holies the greater. 3. Rejoice, ark gilded by the Spirit. 4. Rejoice, inexhaustible treasury of life. 5. Rejoice, honored crown of pious kings. 6. Rejoice, holy pride of devout priests. 7. Rejoice, unshaken tower of the Church. 8. Rejoice, invincible wall of the kingdom. 9. Rejoice, through whom victories are raised up. 10. Rejoice, through whom enemies fall. 11. Rejoice, healing of my body. 12. Rejoice, salvation of my soul.
